title: What does Kentucky need from new AD after Mitch Barnhart retires?
description: An athletics director's job description has changed a lot since Mitch Barnhart started at UK in 2002. What do the Wildcats want from his replacement?
fullText:
On the first day of SEC spring meetings, Kentucky women's basketball coach Kenny Brooks received two greeting cards to sign — one funny, one sentimental.

Upon opening the funny card and seeing a donkey cutout leap off the cardstock, Brooks let out a cheeky laugh. He scribbled a little something next to the burro. Then another little something in its more sincere counterpart. Brooks' notes joined several others wishing Mitch Barnhart well in retirement.

Barnhart, UK's longtime athletics director, will step away at the end of June. His retirement comes after 24 years at the helm of all things Wildcats. Kentucky looks to find a replacement by then, with whomever does succeed Barnhart serving as AD and chief executive officer of Champions Blue, LLC.

The job description has changed a lot since Barnhart started in 2002. SEC commissioner Greg Sankey talked with reporters about it last month at the Hilton Sandestin. There are more people to manage, greater financial figures at stake, heavier emphasis on capital expenditures than the once-super-salient facility arms race and increased investment (at least publicly) of politicians federal and otherwise.

It's a lot for one person to manage. The list of wants and needs is exhaustive. What are folks at Kentucky prioritizing? What do other current ADs feel it takes to succeed in the role nowadays?

Ask Brooks what he wants to see in his new boss, and he'll keep it refreshingly real:

"Well, if I had my druthers, it would be somebody who is really, really a strong advocate for women's basketball — who is just a fanatic for women's basketball."

Of course the women's basketball coach would say that. But in all seriousness, Brooks expressed concern to The Courier Journal for the well-being of the entire department — not just football and men's basketball. Women's basketball is about as successful as it's ever been under Brooks. The women's volleyball team is consistently winning SEC titles and contending for national championships. Gymnastics has been a top 10-15 team for the better part of the last 10 years.

"They (the next AD) got some big shoes to fill," Brooks said. "Kentucky's athletic department is very successful across a lot of venues. Just hope to continue the excitement and the resources there for sports to be successful. Obviously we get into our own little bubble and just start thinking about who we are and what we need, but what Mitch did for 24 years has just been remarkable. So hopefully someone can come in and keep the train rolling."

Football coach Will Stein expressed his love for Barnhart, who hired the Louisville native about three months before announcing his retirement. "He changed my life forever," Stein told The Courier Journal. He hopes whoever replaces Barnhart also "knows everybody's name," cares about athlete well-being and acts as "a servant leader." But Stein also reinforced the importance of having someone who's adept at building up UK's revenue.

Men's basketball coach Mark Pope praised Barnhart as well. During Pope's third SEC spring meetings, he told The Courier Journal that rooms go quiet at the Hilton Sandestin when Barnhart walks in. A sign of respect. It'll be "impossible," Pope said, to replace the man who hired him to coach his alma mater.

Sankey started with the SEC as associate commissioner in 2002. Back then, Barnhart was the league's freshest face. Florida's Jeremy Foley was a young vet. Doug Dickey was still at Tennessee. Vince Dooley still represented UGA. Frank Broyles, who led Arkansas to the SEC, still presided over the Razorbacks.

"What a room," Sankey said, reminiscing.

While many may shrug at the emphasis his league places on tradition and intentionality across various marketing campaigns, Sankey pointed toward it as an important part of preserving the SEC's culture amid administrative turnover. These annual spring meetings have been held at the same location (other than those impacted by COVID-19 in 2020 and 2021) since 1985. The league's elder statesmen and women share their institutional knowledge with new compatriots, so nothing important is lost to time.

"We have a stronger culture today," Sankey said during his May 28 news conference, "even after the change, as we had then (when Sankey started). That doesn't happen by accident. That's a responsibility of our athletics directors, responsibility of us all.

"And the affection shown for Mitch in early May by his colleagues, and Mitch shared his affection for them and for the league, we didn't record that. That's one of the rewards of the work, to see that — competitors sharing that kind of appreciation and respect for a colleague. And today, actually, (Tennessee) chancellor (Donde) Plowman, in the combined meeting, is the one who stopped and said, 'Hey, this is gonna be Mitch's final meeting,' and that respect and appreciation showed again. Those are pretty cool moments to be part of."

Not everything about Barnhart's tenure has been entirely positive, though. He's been named in numerous Title IX lawsuits against the school claiming gender discrimination and sexual abuse. Under his supervision, ex-football coach Mark Stoops was able to secure a contract that guaranteed him nearly $38 million from UK upon his firing (which will be spread out in quarterly payments through April 1, 2031, after negotiations).

And just last month, Barnhart declined the post-retirement executive-in-residence role baked into his contract after immense pushback over the $950,000 salary that would've come with the position. His announcement came two days after Gov. Andy Beshear voiced his displeasure at paying Barnhart such a lucrative amount for a gig with "no defined duties."

The Courier Journal has been engaged in discussion with UK Athletics representatives about scheduling a one-on-one interview with Barnhart since December. He has yet to be made available.

UK president Eli Capiluto said in a release that he's considering "both sitting college athletics directors and people with varied backgrounds in pro sports and business as potential next leaders." At one time, it seemed possible that UK would hire two people to replace Barnhart. At a Champions Blue meeting last month, the board looked at multiple organization models developed by consulting firm Deloitte, and two proposals suggested naming a chief commercialization and growth officer to share responsibilities with a more sports-minded AD.

Search firm DSG Global is aiding UK in the job search, the Lexington Herald-Leader reported. As part of a "listening tour," Capiluto has conducted more than 80 interviews with university senior staff, coaches, donors and other supporters and business leaders. The job posting says whoever fills this CEO/AD role will report directly to Capiluto and work in tandem with other departments, including Information Technology Services, Marketing and Strategic Communication, Student Success and Philanthropy.

This individual would also serve as a non-voting member of the Champions Blue Board of Governors. The LLC was created last year to give UK Athletics flexibility to move more quickly than university bureaucracy allows as a way to better navigate the current, ever-changing landscape of college sports.

Capiluto said in a release that the "chief attributes in the next leader will include:

"A commitment to student success, integrity, compliance and the pursuit of championships

"Financial and commercial acumen to administer larger budgets, generate more revenue and manage expenses

"A capacity to analyze costs and consequences of alternative revenue strategies

"Demonstrated skills in leading the development of a successful approach to athlete compensation, revenue sharing and NIL opportunities, working closely with the institution’s multi-media rights partner, JMI Sports

"An ability to effectively communicate in a complex media environment as well as with other critical constituencies, such as donors, fans, the UK Board of Trustees and Champions Blue Board of Governors

"A willingness to work collaboratively with a team of leaders, utilizing shared services across the UK enterprise to achieve unit and strategic goals"

No matter who takes over UK Athletics, they will have their work cut out for them in navigating the rapidly evolving landscape of college sports.

Reach college sports enterprise reporter Payton Titus at ptitus@gannett.com and follow her on X @petitus25. 

This article originally appeared on Louisville Courier Journal: Kentucky AD search: What UK looks for in Mitch Barnhart's replacement

title: How Louisville's Skylar Meade coached Troy baseball to super regionals
description: As a player, Skylar Meade helped Louisville to its first College World Series appearance in 2007. Now a coach, he hopes to do the same with Troy.
fullText:
Before Troy baseball’s NCAA Tournament super regional appearance, head coach Skylar Meade will give his team a message using Louisville coach Dan McDonnell’s words.

“Your story’s already written,” Meade recalled his former college coach saying nearly 20 years ago. “If you decide to write another chapter, then it's going to be the greatest one ever written.”

The young McDonnell, who Meade described as brash and confident, yet motivational, was a first-year head coach when he led Louisville to a home super regional before earning the program’s first College World Series appearance in 2007. His words during the magical run were just as applicable then as they are now for Meade, who was a senior that season and is now leading the Trojans on their own memorable postseason run.

Troy has made the regionals four other times over the past 15 years but never made it out of the first weekend. During Meade’s second year in 2023, the Trojans had their first 40-win season and made the tournament. But they went 1-2 in the Tuscaloosa, Alabama, regional before missing the postseason the next two years. This year, Troy is making the program’s first-ever super regional appearance — with the added bonus of playing at home.

“This is what we've been striving to do,” Meade said. “Honestly, being fortunate enough to host a super regional, it's like a whole other level, but the game rewards you sometimes if you can handle all the ups and downs, and so we'll embrace it this weekend and beyond. It's gonna be a lot of fun.”

The Ballard High School graduate and the Trojans (36-30) will host Little Rock in the super regional series, which is set to begin at 4 p.m. Friday at Riddle-Pace Field in Troy, Alabama. Game 2 will take place at 2 p.m. Saturday, and a Game 3 time will be announced later, if needed. An uber-confident Little Rock team comes into the series on an eight-game winning streak after taking down regional host Southern Miss, then Jacksonville State twice.

Meade credits scheduling with helping Troy get to this point. The Trojans played seven games against ranked opponents, including a series against Sun Belt Conference foe Southern Miss, and went 4-3 in those contests. Their best win was a 12-inning, 6-5 victory over Georgia (now the top remaining national seed at No. 3), on Feb. 25. But there was still room for improvement as Troy battled with injuries while trying to get everyone in the right headspace.

That moment came on May 5 when the squad beat 17th-ranked Alabama, 6-1, then took a 2-1 series win over rival South Alabama that weekend. The Trojans won five of their last six regular-season games and made the Sun Belt Conference Tournament semifinals. They were the last team in the NCAA Tournament field; their 32-29 record on Selection Monday was worst among at-large qualifiers.

In many ways, Troy’s journey to the supers reminds Meade of Louisville’s 2007 team. For one, both squads had to get through Miami to earn at least two more games at home. The 2007 Cardinals beat the ‘Canes twice before taking down Missouri. The 2026 Trojans lost to Miami to open the tournament, then won a rematch in the elimination bracket. Ironically, Chris Dominguez, who was also part of Louisville’s first CWS squad, was across the field from Meade as one of Miami’s assistant coaches.

Meade and his father caught up with Dominguez during the weekend. There was no doubt in Meade’s mind that he and his former teammate would end up right where they are now. It’s what they envisioned for themselves 19 years ago when making history at Louisville.

Meade hopes to help Troy’s players experience the same feelings he and Dominguez did by earning the program’s first CWS appearance. The journey to Omaha and another chapter of the Trojans’ story is continuing to be written.

“It's a big deal if you're part of history,” Meade said, “but the great part about being part of history (is) it's not like there’s a ceiling as to what all becomes of it. I don't want these guys to feel like we're done, because we're not.”

Reach Louisville football, women's basketball and baseball beat writer Alexis Cubit at acubit@gannett.com and follow her on X at @Alexis_Cubit.

This article originally appeared on Louisville Courier Journal: Louisville's Skylar Meade leads Troy baseball to NCAA super regionals

title: Mexico thrash Serbia 5-1 ahead of World Cup opener
description: World Cup co-hosts Mexico have completed their tune-up schedule for the tournament with a 5-1 thrashing of Serbia on Thursday evening. The Serbian team took the lead through Petar Stanic, but goals from Johan Vásquez and an own goal from Stefan Bukinac gave Mexico the lead before the break.
fullText:
World Cup co-hosts Mexico have completed their tune-up schedule for the tournament with a 5-1 thrashing of Serbia on Thursday evening.

The Serbian team took the lead through Petar Stanic, but goals from Johan Vásquez and an own goal from Stefan Bukinac gave Mexico the lead before the break.

Raul Jimenez, another own goal from Adem Advic and Luis Chavez completed the score in the second half.

Mexico are hosting the World Cup with the United States and Canada, and kick off the tournament against South Africa on June 11. In Group A, they also face the Czech Republic and South Korea.

title: German survey says public don't believe team can win World Cup
description: The vast majority of Germans do not expect the national team to win the upcoming World Cup, according to a poll released on Friday. According to the ZDF political survey Politbarometer, only 15% believe the German team will triumph at the tournament in the United States, Canada and Mexico, while 72% are doubtful.
fullText:
The vast majority of Germans do not expect the national team to win the upcoming World Cup, according to a poll released on Friday.

According to the ZDF political survey Politbarometer, only 15% believe the German team will triumph at the tournament in the United States, Canada and Mexico, while 72% are doubtful.

After the team's group-stage exits at the two previous tournaments, 3% believe Germany will again be knocked out before the round of 16.

Some 15% expect an exit in the last-16 round, while 33% expect the team to reach the quarter-finals.

A further 15% believe Germany will go out in the semi-finals and 2% expect a defeat in the final on July 19.

The World Cup will feature 48 teams for the first time, with the knockout phase beginning with 32 teams for the first time.

The German squad led by head coach Julian Nagelsmann kicks off on June 14 against outsiders Curaçao.

Germany's other group-stage opponents are Ivory Coast and Ecuador. On Saturday, Germany has a World Cup warm-up match against co-host United States in Chicago.

The poll was conducted by the Mannheim-based research group Wahlen between June 1-3, with 1,274 people surveyed.

title: Michigan has 7 of the 200 best modern golf courses in America
description: It's no surprise to see Michigan all over Golfweek's 2026 ranking of the best modern golf courses in the U.S. See which ones made it.
fullText:
Michigan has seven of the top 200 modern courses in the United States, according to Golfweek.

It's no surprise to see Michigan all over the 2026 ranking, released June 1 – The Mitten is one of the very best states for golf in America (and it might be the best when you factor in value).

Courses must be opened since 1960, and have a minimum of 25 votes to qualify for this list from Golfweek – which, like the Free Press, is part of USA TODAY Co.

Why 1960? Golfweek says, in part, the "design and construction techniques for courses shifted fundamentally" after 1960 with the popularization of the sport soaring.

Golfweek's panel of hundreds of raters must have had rounds played in the past 10 years for ratings to be included in the compilations.

Here's a look at the seven Michigan courses built since 1960 to make Golfweek's premium list, as rated by the publication, along with more information about each course.

Architect(s): Rick Smith, Warren Henderson.

Marlowe says: Yet to play it, but The Loop is a first-of-its-kind reversible 18 by the acclaimed Tom Doak. Forest Dunes is a must visit.

Marlowe says: Every golfer should experience this place at least once. There is nothing like it.

[ Greywalls golf course in Marquette feels like a time warp back to Jurassic era ]

Architect(s): Warren Henderson, Rick Smith.

Marlowe says: Golf, lake views, sunsets, sandy soil. What more could you ask for to build and play a golf course? It's the long-reigning No. 1-rated public course in Michigan for a reason.

For more, find Golfweek's entire top 200 ranking.

Marlowe Alter is an Assistant Sports Editor at the Detroit Free Press. Email him at: malter@freepress.com.

title: Who is the Vance Law Firm Softball and Baseball Player of the Year for the 2026 season?
description: See who won the Montgomery Advertiser's Softball and Baseball Player of the Year polls from the 2026 season, sponsored by the Vance Law Firm.
fullText:
The results are in for the Montgomery Advertiser's athlete of the week, presented by the Vance Law Firm, for performances during May 18-22.

The poll ran from May 24-28, with readers given the option to vote once every hour.

Prattville's Haylee Jackson won the softball poll after leading the Lions in the batting and pitching for the 2026 season. She finished the season with a .405 batting average with 37 RBIs on 32 hits with 10 home runs. In the circle, she went 7-4 with 60 strikeouts and a 2.20 ERA.

Jackson won the poll with 49% of the total votes, beating out Brewbaker Tech's Kaymarria Bandy who received 43% of the total votes.

Prattville's Dorsett Jackson won the baseball poll after an impressive 2026 season, hitting an outstanding .615 with 27 RBIs on 32 hits.

Dorsett won the poll with 62% of the total votes, beating out Pike Road's Quinton Robinson, who received 36% of the total votes.

Nominees come from season's top performers the week before. To nominate a top performer, have your team's coach or stat keeper email sports@montgomeryadvertiser.com by 11 p.m. CT each Friday. Voting opens Monday and lasts until noon Thursday. Winners are announced Fridays.

title: Justin Lebron's down year with Alabama baseball explained, and why he's still a first-rounder
description: Justin Lebron's down year with Alabama baseball as explained by his hitting coach, and why the shortstop is still a first-round steal.
fullText:
After the Tennessee series, Alabama baseball shortstop Justin Lebron tried to get down to the bottom of the slump he has experienced in his junior season with the Crimson Tide.

Entering the Tuscaloosa Super Regional, Lebron has driven in 46 runs and notched 16 homers on the season with a .274 clip. That wouldn't draw too much criticism in the SEC — if he wasn't the same Justin Lebron that hit .360 for 18 home runs and 72 RBIs one season prior.

Lebron also leads Alabama in errors, which he did last year. However, he has 18 ahead of the supers opener against St. John's on Saturday, June 6. In 2025, he had just nine.

The series opener against Tennessee saw Lebron extend a 12-game hit streak, but it snapped in Game 2 as Lebron went 0 for 4. That signaled it was time for a call with his hitting coach, Aaron Vorachek, who was also his high school coach in Florida at Archbishop McCarthy.

REQUIRED READING: Why best version of Justin Lebron could take Alabama baseball to Omaha

"I saw some things swing-wise," Vorachek told The Tuscaloosa News. The biggest thing he noticed, though, was how rushed Lebron looked.

"I said, 'You're cutting off the swing a little bit where instead of his hands working through the middle, his hands are starting to work to third so he gets some rollover," Vorachek explained.

Lebron told Vorachek that pitchers were throwing him a lot of right-on-right changeups and sliders, which was causing him to be off on fastballs.

They discussed Lebron's approach to the plate, plus direction and timing. Since, Vorachek has noticed improvements, but he continues to find Lebron to be too quick with it.

"Even this weekend, I still think his timing was a bit rushed," Vorachek said, reflecting on Alabama's 3-0 region championship sweep. "When he's late with his timing, his swing looks rushed and a little off-balanced."

"Whatever organization he's with, I just feel like the more that they keep working on that, the more you're gonna see a consistent Justin Lebron," Vorachek said.

Vorachek explained to Lebron that he's not just the lows of this season, instead describing him as an "entire body of work." He doesn't see the decline impacting his stock in the MLB draft, either.

"The crazy thing is everybody talks about the drop off, and obviously, there's pressure, right?" Vorachek said. "If you look at the box score, he usually has a hit a game. If your funk is one hit a game, that's a good funk to be in."

In May, Lebron's only game without a hit was in a 3-1 loss to Ole Miss.

Vorachek describes Lebron as a "unicorn."

"Roch Cholowsky at UCLA doesn't steal bases like Justin does," Vorachek said. "I'm like, 'There's nobody in the country with your power and your speed.'"

Even with the tweaks that need fixing, to Vorachek, MLB scouts are still liking what they see. Colorado Rockies scout Zach Zulli called up Vorachek in awe of Lebron's "'elite swing.'"

"A lot of the organizations are very analytical now. But there are still a lot of good organizations with good baseball people that are making those baseball decisions," Vorachek said. "I think that they can look past that and say, 'This guy's a dude.'"

Emilee Smarr covers Alabama basketball and Crimson Tide athletics for The Tuscaloosa News. She can be reached via email at esmarr@usatodayco.com.

This article originally appeared on The Tuscaloosa News: Justin Lebron still MLB draft 'unicorn' despite Alabama baseball slump

title: The Phillies adding Mike Trout would be great, but it seems unlikely
description: The Philadelphia Phillies acquiring Mike Trout seems unlikely, but he would be better than the current outfield.
fullText:
The Phillies adding Mike Trout would be great, but it seems unlikely originally appeared on The Sporting News. Add The Sporting News as a Preferred Source by clicking here.

One of the more well-known secrets surrounding the Philadelphia Phillies is that they will be looking to add a right-handed bat at the August 3 trade deadline.

The Phillies have seen little production from their outfield, and their offseason signing, Adolis Garcia, has not performed well overall (.196 batting average and .591 OPS). However, Garcia has gotten off to a good start in June, collecting three hits (one double and a home run) in 10 at-bats.

Despite Garcia's recent success, Phillies right fielders are 23rd in OPS (.625) and slugging percentage (.339). The rest of the outfield hasn't been better, with Phillies left fielders ranking 17th-best in OPS (.693) and their center fielders at 14th in MLB (.662).

All the rumors surrounding the Phillies at the trade deadline have Millville, New Jersey native Mike Trout returning home. However, Trout would need to okay a trade, given his status as a 10-and-5 player and a full no-trade clause.

More MLB News:CJ Abrams can make Nationals/Expos history with All-Star nod

According to Bleacher Report's Tim Kelly, he doesn't think the 11-time All-Star will be traded to the Phillies; rather, he believes Trout's teammate, Jo Adell, is a likely candidate to land with the Phillies.

"We're inclined to think Trout won't want to be traded, but if he does, the Phillies might become the most likely landing spot given that the Millville, NJ native grew up as a Phillie sports fan," writes Kelly.

"The Phillies could also consider trying to acquire a piece they think could take another step forward and become a star in their organization, like [Jo] Adell or Heliot Ramos [San Francisco Giants]."

Trout would bring more power to the Phillies, mashing 14 home runs to the Phillies' outfield's combined 12. Trout also has a strong ability to get on base, leading Major League Baseball in walks, with 60. This is 16 more than the Phillies outfielders: Garcia (20), Brandon Marsh (10), and Justin Crawford (14) have collectively.

Having the three-time MVP would give the Philles someone to protect Bryce Harper and Kyle Schwarber. Schwarber has been batting leadoff for the Phillies as of late, but adding Trout would allow him to slide back into the two-hole, where he's been productive (batting .247 with a 1.020 OPS and 21 home runs in 182 at-bats).

title: Churchill Downs set to unveil a new statue of a historic jockey
description: Oliver Lewis won the first Kentucky Derby. Now, Churchill Downs will cement his place in horseracing history with a statue outside the First Turn Club.
fullText:
Oliver Lewis won the first Kentucky Derby.

For more than a century, those seven words largely remained as the only fact known about the jockey who rode Aristides to victory in 1875.

Now the rider — a former slave who was 18 when he made history — will be commemorated on horse racing's most hallowed ground, beneath the twin spires at Churchill Downs.

Churchill Downs will unveil a statue of Lewis, designed by his descendants and based off one of the only existing photos of Lewis, on June 24 at 11 a.m. with a dedication ceremony in the Clubhouse Gardens.

It is believed to be the first statue of a black jockey at a horse racing track in the U.S.

More: Oliver Lewis won the first Kentucky Derby atop Aristides. His story doesn't end there

When Rodney Van Johnson, the great-great-grandson of Oliver Lewis, and his uncle, David Johnson, visited Churchill Downs on the first Saturday in May just two years ago, it was the first time a member of their family had stepped foot at the track since the first Kentucky Derby.

Lewis again rode Aristides in 1875, this time in the Belmont Stakes, finishing second, but Henry Price McGrath, the horse's then-owner and a notorious gambler, picked a different jockey to ride the champion horse at other big races.

Aristides is already cemented in history outside the main gates of Churchill Downs, with the twin spires framing his likeness in full stride.

In the lead up to the 150th Kentucky Derby two years ago, The Courier Journal told the story of that very first race in a way it had never been told before: from the viewpoint of the first jockey to win it all. In response, Lewis' family was invited to Louisville as part of festivities in the run-up to the big race.

The idea for a permanent reminder of his great-great-grandfather took hold of Van Johnson while at the track in 2024. Of the first 28 Kentucky Derbys, 15 were won by Black jockeys. But as segregation swept through the nation, Black jockeys were pushed out of the industry. The last Derby to be won by a Black jockey was in 1902 by Jimmy Winkfield.

Van Johnson didn't want it be another anniversary year before the public celebrated not just the horse but also the jockey of the race that started it all.

"I thought that pretty much, if we build it, we will get it placed," Van Johnson told The Courier Journal. "I wanted to have a statue made and have it for our family because what Oliver did is such a huge accomplishment."

Van Johnson created the Oliver Lewis Foundation, which preserves the legacy of Lewis beyond his time as a jockey and includes his work as an entrepreneur and innovator. Lewis is credited with creating the modern day racing form.

The foundation designed the statue based on a photo of Lewis standing next to a fence post, the only known photo of him from around the age he was when he won the Derby. Van Johnson figured if Churchill Downs wouldn't accept the Lewis statue, he'd ask the city of Cincinnati, where Lewis and his family eventually relocated, to place the piece of history somewhere in its city limits.

Lewis was part of Cincinnati history too, living in the Kenyon-Barr neighborhood. Cincinnati's Urban Renewal Department wanted to renew a part of the city that was seen as "the slums," a thriving neighborhood with thousands of establishments ― from churches and restaurants to other local businesses. It was Cincinnati's Harlem. And the story of its demolition can be summed up as this: It is the largest displacement of families in American history, with 25,000 people, including Lewis' family, displaced.

But Van Johnson thought Lewis' impact on history would best be on display where he created history.

"So much history is lost in the horse racing industry," Van Johnson said. "Black jockeys were moved out because of racism. It was very sad. So, I think it’s going to be a great opportunity. This is going to be a forever mark that so many young kids and people will get to know."

Stephanie Kuzydym is an enterprise and investigative reporter. She can be reached at skuzydym@courier-journal.com. Follow her at @stephkuzy.

This article originally appeared on Louisville Courier Journal: Churchill Downs Oliver Lewis first Kentucky Derby winner jockey statue

title: Padres' Manny Machado doesn't mince his words for the team's poor play
description: The San Diego Padres have been terrible as of last and third baseman Manny Machado didn't hold back on how he felt the team was performing.
fullText:
Padres' Manny Machado doesn't mince his words for the team's poor play originally appeared on The Sporting News. Add The Sporting News as a Preferred Source by clicking here.

The San Diego Padres leave Philadelphia knowing the Phillies have their number, sweeping them for the second time in 10 days. The Phillies' six-game season sweep of the Padres is the second time the Phillies have swept the Friars in a six-game season series, doing so in 2005 (July 22-25 and August 12-14).

The loss against the Phillies is the latest in the Padres woes, as they have won one game in their last 10 and have been outscored 45-26 in that span. As frustrations began to mount, third baseman Manny Machado didn't mince his words when talking about how bad the Padres are.

“People are frustrated,” Machado told MLB.com's AJ Cassavell. “The fans want us to win games. So do we. We’re more frustrated than anybody. We want to turn things around. Everybody on this ballclub is trying to do that. So we’re going to keep working, just know that we’re still in a good position, even though we’re playing like [expletive].

“But things will turn around. It’s a long season. There’s still a lot of baseball to be played. This group in here knows it. All we can do is keep working. Things will change.”

More MLB News:CJ Abrams can make Nationals/Expos history with All-Star nod

The Padres are the worst team in Major League Baseball in scoring runs (235), getting on base (.291) and only the New York Mets are worse at slugging the baseball (.359), with the Padres one point ahead (.360).

Machado hasn't helped the Padres during their struggles, as the seven-time All-Star has been ice-cold. In the 15 games, Machado is batting .148 with 18 strikeouts in 54 at-bats, but has five home runs for a .444 slugging percentage.

Machado did offer a positive message in all the bitterness: “I don’t think it can get worse,” said a sullen Manny Machado from the visiting clubhouse in Philadelphia. “I mean, there’s only one way: Up.”

The Padres begin a three-game series with the Mets on Friday, who have played .500 baseball in their last 10 games. Facing the worst team in the National League East (27-35), this series could get the Padres back on track.

title: Meet Troy baseball coach Skylar Meade: 3 things to know
description: Meet Troy's baseball coach Skylar Meade with these three things to know about the Trojan skipper ahead of the NCAA Tournament Super Regional.
fullText:
Troy baseball is playing in the first Super Regional in program history, and it's at home.

Troy came out of the losers bracket of the Gainesville Regional and beat Florida twice to claim the regional title as the No. 3 team. Little Rock advanced out of the Hattiesburg Regional as the four-seed, sweeping through the regional by beating Southern Miss and Jax State twice.

The Super Regional starts at 4 p.m. CT Friday on ESPNU and Saturday's matchup is set for 2 p.m. on ESPN2. The time for the if-needed Sunday matchup is TBD.

Get to know Trojans coach Skylar Meade ahead of the series.

Troy Baseball: Can Troy baseball advance to College World Series? Troy-Little Rock Super Regional prediction

Regional Champ: How Troy baseball won Gainesville Regional in magical week

Meade came to Troy from South Carolina where he spent three seasons as the pitching coach. He also has coached at Michigan State, Middle Tennessee State and Eastern Illinois. It was with Eastern Illinois that he got his start as a graduate assistant and earned his master's degree.

Meade was a key piece of Louisville's run to the College World Series in 2007. He won nine games as a left-handed pitcher. That season, he held batters to a .247 average during the run. According to his Troy bio, he made the final out against Mississippi State that year.

The Trojans have made the super regional for the first time in program history this season under Meade. It is the second appearance for the Trojans in the NCAA Tournament under Meade. He won 32 games his first season with Troy, the most since 2019. He won 40 games in 2023 and at least 36 in every year since.

Maxwell Donaldson covers high school sports, Jax State athletics, the outdoors in Alabama and more for the Gadsden Times and USA TODAY Network. Find him on Twitter/X @_Max_Donaldson and contact him at MDonaldson@usatodayco.com.

This article originally appeared on Montgomery Advertiser: 3 things to know about Troy baseball coach Skylar Meade

title: Maverick Vinales on MotoGP future and injury recovery: “I don’t have to convince anyone”
description: With his future still hanging in the balance, Vinales has no doubts that he can return to full strength and earn another MotoGP deal
fullText:
Maverick Vinales insists he ‘doesn’t have to convince anyone’ that he will make a full recovery from his shoulder injury and deserves another MotoGP contract.

A violent highside during last July’s German Grand Prix qualifying continues to plague Vinales almost 12 months on. While the Tech3 rider entered the 2026 season feeling he was fully fit again, persistent issues forced him to withdraw from the third race in Austin and undergo another surgery on his left shoulder.

After missing further races in Jerez and Le Mans, he returned to action in Barcelona last month, but continues to face significant physical limitations.

Heading into this weekend’s Hungarian Grand Prix, he has just one points finish to his name from 2026 - an 11th place finish in Catalan.

Vinales’ fitness issues have come during a crucial stage in the silly season, with only a handful of seats remaining on the rider market. KTM is understood to have signed Alex Marquez and Fabio di Giannantonio for the factory team, while Vinales, Brad Binder and Senna Agius are all vying for the two Tech3 seats.

Asked whether he could offer an update on where he would be racing in 2027, the Spaniard said: “Not yet, and I cannot tell too much [because] I don't know.”

Pressed further if his 2027 chances depended upon his injury recovery, he added: “No, the shoulder will be good, I mean the shoulder is a matter of [time].”

Vinales was initially considered a shoo-in for a factory promotion in 2027, with KTM earmarking him as a direct replacement for Ducati-bound Pedro Acosta.

However, amid his poor form and lingering shoulder problems early in the year, the Austrian factory instead opted to recruit riders from outside its roster.

Asked if KTM believed he would be able to recover quickly and regain his form, Vinales pointed to his impressive results in 2025 prior to the shoulder injury, including a provisional second place in the Qatar GP that was later stripped due to a tyre pressure penalty.

“I don't have to convince nobody,” he insisted. “When I was okay at KTM, in that moment, I was the leader. Then, okay, I got injured, but I was in the Red Bull Athlete Performance Centre, and they also agree that I will recover 100%.

“Obviously, it's not easy because it's been seven months that I haven’t used some muscles. It needs a bit of time. But what is clear is that I'm gonna recover 100%.

“The biggest [question] was that one: if, after the second operation, I was able to recover. But I am able to recover and that's the point.”

Vinales added he is targeting a return to full fitness by the Czech GP on 21 June.

“Good recovery after a really hard weekend in Mugello,” he said. “On Monday, I was completely fatigued, but after that my body recovered. I feel I have good energy now.

“I think Mugello was fantastic to keep improving the strength, especially on the bike to be able to be faster and stronger.

“Let's see how I feel on Friday. But after Friday, I have two weeks to fully work on my strength. So for me, my target is to be full strength in Brno.”

Moto2 points leader Manu Gonzalez knocking on Trackhouse's door for 2027 MotoGP seat

Cal Crutchlow to ride through shoulder injury in “challenging” Balaton Park weekend

To read more Motorsport.com articles visit our website.

title: Ole! Here are some places to watch the World Cup in Jacksonville
description: Sure, you could watch the World Cup at home, but where's the fun in that? Join the crowd at one of these Jacksonville places that'll air the matches.
fullText:
When looking for a great place to watch futbol in the Jacksonville area, think football. With the World Cup coming to North America, fans will gather to cheer on their favorite soccer teams and, in many cases, they’ll do it at the same places they gather to watch the Jaguars and the Gators.

Just about any bar with a television and a license to show the matches is likely to have one on, and some places will cater to fans of a specific national team. This year’s tourney is being played in the U.S., Canada and Mexico, so you won’t even have to get up in the middle of the night to see your favorite team.

Here are some places that plan on catering to World Cup fans throughout the tournament, which will include 104 matches running from June 11 to July 19.

World Cup 2026: What every soccer fan needs to know, from A to Z

7520 Quarter Mill Drive, theblockjax.com

The outdoor food hall/dog park/music pavilion/kids’ playground has a huge LED wall that will be put to good use during the World Cup. Dozens of matches are on the schedule, with at least one showing nearly every day. While you’re there, enjoy the fare at one of the eight restaurants at The Block.

600 Park St. No. 10, and 1040 San Jose Blvd., playersgrille.com

Players now has two locations for watching the World Cup, one in Mandarin that opened in 2023 and a newer location inside the Home 2 Suites by Hilton hotel off Interstate 95 in the historic Brooklyn neighborhood. Both offer a full kitchen and plenty of TVs for watching your favorite matches.

111 Beach Blvd, Jacksonville Beach, (904) 482-1000, sneakerssportgrille.com

With 10 jumbo TV screens and a zillion smaller ones, you won’t miss any action. They also have a dog-friendly outdoor patio with its own screens, in case Fido’s a big fan. Spanish-language matches will be shown at the nearby Ajua Mexican Kitchen & Bar.

1850 N. Main St., (904) 475-2970; 1618 Penman Road, Jacksonville Beach, (904) 746-7933, stringssportsbrewery.com

Kick back with a Bullet Bob hazy IPA at the place that was voted as Jacksonville’s favorite local brewery. They have a ton of TVs at both the Springfield location, built into an old service station, and at the Beaches, where Strings is housed in an old general store.

6460 U.S. 1, St. Augustine, (904) 257-3211, thewobblywhale.com

Fans of the British and Scottish teams might feel at home at the Wobbly Whale, which is housed in a cottage and features European fare and pints of Guinness and other ales. It’s not a sports bar, but if the televisions are on, they are showing sports.

495 Durbin Pavilion Drive, St. Johns, (904) 946-7719, walk-ons.com

You’d be hard-pressed to find a seat that doesn’t have a view of at least a few of the 55 televisions at Walk-ons, part of a national chain co-owned by NFL Hall of Famer Drew Brees. Walk-ons started in Louisiana, so expect a bit of spice on the menu.

This article originally appeared on Florida Times-Union: Best bars to watch the World Cup in Jacksonville

title: “I’ve been working hard on different parts of my game” – Braiden Graham plots pre-season push to impress Moyes
description: (Photo by Cameron Smith/Getty Images)Braiden Graham has said he wants to keep improving and break into the Everton first team, after earning his first international call up this month.Graham has been ...
fullText:
(Photo by Cameron Smith/Getty Images)

Braiden Graham has said he wants to keep improving and break into the Everton first team, after earning his first international call up this month.

Graham has been included in the Northern Ireland squad for this month's camp and was an unused substitute during the 1-0 win over Guinea on Thursday. He could make his international debut against France on Monday night.

The 18-year-old's inclusion is reward for his prolific exploits at youth level, where he scored 26 goals for Everton's u-21 side in 2025/26 to win the Under-21s' Player of the Season award.

Graham has also scored prolifically for the national team at youth level, including nine goals in 13 Under-19 appearances for Northern Ireland.

He now has the chance to impress Michael O'Neill with the national team, before his senior debut for Everton. However, Graham has his sights set firmly on winning David Moyes's trust in pre-season.

“I’ve had a good season and enjoyed it – scoring goals and playing well – and I’m really happy to receive the Under-21s Player of the Season award,” Graham told evertontv.

“I’ve been working hard on different parts of my game – on and off the pitch – so I’m happy I’ve kicked on this season and done well.

“I’ll keep working hard in the off-season and then come back and hope to have a good pre-season and kick on – maybe go on loan or stay here.”

title: West Ham’s Mohamadou Kanté attracts Ligue 1 interest
description: Mohamadou Kanté (20) could leave West Ham United this summer and a return to his native France is an option, as per a report from The Athletic. Kanté joined West Ham from Paris FC back in 2024. La...
fullText:
Mohamadou Kanté (20) could leave West Ham United this summer and a return to his native France is an option, as per a report from The Athletic.

Kanté joined West Ham from Paris FC back in 2024. Last season, he made the breakthrough into the first team. He made 11 appearances in the Premier League, although he only accumulated 66 minutes of playing time in the league. However, he did make starts in the FA Cup.

With West Ham’s relegation to the EFL Championship confirmed, there is expected to be an exodus in the summer.

Kanté is a player who is expected to be of interest to clubs. West Ham are bracing themselves for bids. The Athletic understands that the midfielder, valued between £15-20m, is attracting interest from Ligue 1 sides.

The publication adds that West Ham will also listen to offers for France internationals Alphonse Areola and Jean-Clair Todibo.

title: Sean O'Malley looking to knock Aiemann Zahabi out at the White House
description: Former bantamweight champion Sean O'Malley requested a fight against sixth-ranked Aiemann Zahabi for his last fight. He got Song Yadong instead. Zahabi picked up a win over former titlte challenger Marlon Vera in his last outing and called out O'Malley. The two will meet on the UFC Freedom 250 main ...
fullText:
Former bantamweight champion Sean O'Malley requested a fight against sixth-ranked Aiemann Zahabi for his last fight. He got Song Yadong instead. Zahabi picked up a win over former titlte challenger Marlon Vera in his last outing and called out O'Malley. The two will meet on the UFC Freedom 250 main card at the White House on June 14.

Zahabi showed up on O'Malley's radar following his UFC 315 win over Brazilian legend and former two-time featherweight titleholder Jose Aldo.

"I think the first time I saw him was when he fought Aldo was the first time I had really heard about him and he was tough, durable, skilled, comes from a good camp. Got another win against 'Chito,' called me out and I was like, I'm down for that fight. I asked for that fight last fight. They gave me Song. Here we are," O'Malley told UFC on Paramount +.

"He's very durable. He's very tough, but that's what's going to get him hurt is how tough he is, how durable he is, and I'm going to land big shots over and over."

O'Malley's end game is to get back to a title fight, but knows that will at least require an impressive performance at the White House.

"I have to finish Zahabi, you know. I can't go out there and get a boring win. Got to go out there and finish him. And even then, it's, you know, we'll see," O'Malley said. "He's very tough, very durable. I'm going to have to land a knockout shot multiple times and, you know, I'm capable of doing that against his style and landing good shots a lot."

title: Farrell extends Ireland contract to 2031
description: Ireland men's head coach Andy Farrell has signed a new contract with the Irish Rugby Football Union (IRFU) until 2031.
fullText:
Ireland men's head coach Andy Farrell has signed a contract extension with the Irish Rugby Football Union (IRFU) until 2031.

The 50-year-old replaced Joe Schmidt after the 2019 World Cup - having spent three years in the New Zealander's backroom - and has led Ireland to two Six Nations titles, including Grand Slam in 2023, and a historic Test series win over the All Blacks in New Zealand.

Farrell temporarily stepped away from the Ireland job at the start of 2025 to take over as British and Irish Lions head coach and the tourists to a Test series win over Australia before returning to Dublin.

Earlier this year, the 2023 World Rugby coach of the year was linked with a return to Saracens, where he previously played and coached, which prompted an angry response from the Prem club.

But speaking in March, Rugby Football Union boss Bill Sweeney said England's governing body was "not in a dialogue" with Farrell.

After autumn defeats by New Zealand and world champions South Africa, a dismal opening Six Nations loss to France in Paris increased the pressure on Farrell, but victories over Italy, England, Wales and Scotland secured the Triple Crown and pushed Les Bleus all the way in pursuit of the championship.

His current contract with the IRFU was due to expire after the 2027 Rugby World Cup, but the former England international will now lead Ireland to the following tournament which will be held in the USA.

Farrell said he was "incredibly proud to continue this journey" with Ireland.

"It is a privilege to work with such a talented group of players and staff, and to represent the supporters whose passion and unwavering support drive this team forward and inspire us every time we take the field," he said.

"What excites me most is the increasing strength and investment in pathways and the quality of talent coming through.

"There is a real confidence in the system that has been built across the provinces and age-grade programmes, and I believe with sustained effort that the best is yet to come."

IRFU chief executive Kevin Potts said the organisation is "delighted" to tie Farrell down to a fresh deal.

"He is a truly world-class coach and an exceptional leader who has helped shape Ireland as one of the most exciting and consistent teams in the world," said Potts.

"His impact reaches far beyond what we see on the pitch – he has instilled a deep sense of pride, connection, inspiration and belief across Irish rugby, creating a culture that truly unites players and supporters and one that Irish Fans at home and across the globe feel incredibly proud to stand behind."

title: Newcastle reach €28m agreement for Reims goalkeeper Ewen Jaouen
description: Newcastle United and Ligue 2 side Stade de Reims have reached a €28.5m agreement over the transfer of France youth goalkeeper Ewen Jaouen (20), according to Canal+. The 20-year-old was a sought-af...
fullText:
Newcastle United and Ligue 2 side Stade de Reims have reached a €28.5m agreement over the transfer of France youth goalkeeper Ewen Jaouen (20), according to Canal+. The 20-year-old was a sought-after prospect. He reportedly visited Chelsea FC’s facilities and Real Madrid once identified him as Thibaut Courtois’ long-term successor, but the next of his career will take him to Tyneside.

Jaouen impressed while on loan at USL Dunkerque during the 2024-2025 campaign, almost leading the club to promotion from Ligue 2 and also taking the Northern club to the semi-finals of the Coupe de France. He returned to parent club Reims last summer, following Les Stadistes’ relegation from Ligue 1.

He has been the No.1 throughout the season for Reims, however, the club have not managed to return to France’s top-flight at the first time of asking. Newcastle were in the market for a young goalkeeper, with Nick Pope having turned 34 in April. The Magpies also parted ways with Aaron Ramsdale recently.

title: Chat is infinite scroll for work
description: Infinite scroll for work
fullText:
A couple months ago, I decided to reverse engineer Notion, pixel for pixel. Just a few prompts while I cooked dinner.

Over the span of eight hours, I maxed out my quotas for Claude and Codex multiple times, as I coaxed the agents to burn millions of tokens, sending incoherent messages in half a dozen chat threads to keep the plates spinning.

At 4am, strung out, with a migraine coming on (and a working prototype), I called it quits.

I don’t use Notion. I’m not trying to build a Notion competitor. But the tokens were there to be spent.

The fatal flaw of chatbots is every message gets a response no matter how stupid it is. They validate every thought, and act on every impulse.

It’s hard to delude yourself TikTok is useful, but chatting with AI feels productive, like you’re a wizard exerting agency over the world.

Over the past 4 months, I’ve shipped 2 native mobile apps, 2 Claude plugins, 4 open source libraries, and helped hundreds of people improve how they delegate to AI.

I was spending an alarming percentage of my time babysitting Claude. If an agent succeeds you get a dopamine hit, and if it fails, you get adrenaline. Both reinforce more usage.

What was most worrying was that the more I chatted with AI, the more I felt a pull towards projects AI is good at, instead of what’s worth working on.

Just as social media removed the stopping point from entertainment, chat removes the stopping point from productivity. Chat is infinite scroll for work.

If AI writes tens of thousands of lines of code I don’t understand, I need AI to help me debug it, which introduces more surface area for bugs, and creates a game of whack-a-mole.

Or worse, erodes my ability to make decisions without feeling paralyzed.

Better models accelerate the problem; compounding screw-ups and encouraging more blind trust.

In 2024, I gave up using (most of) the internet for 5 months.

Throughout the challenge, I jotted down questions about politics, current events, and pop culture I wanted to research once I got back online. At first, the questions gushed out of me, then they turned into a trickle, and then went dry altogether.

At the end of the challenge, I looked at my list, and not a single question felt worth researching. Consumption had been creating the desire to consume.

Last summer, feeling too reliant on chatbots, I went through my last 50 conversations, and realized that none of them needed to be had.

So I went cold turkey, and deleted all my AI tools except Claude Code.

At first, this was alarming, like I was losing a limb. But after a few days, an AI-induced fog started to clear. I felt less paralysis making decisions, and more time in my own rhythm instead of getting sucked into AI’s train of thought.

If you’re a manager and every 5 minutes an employee came to you with a question, your brain would explode. The better the employee, the longer they can work unsupervised.

AI has gotten good enough to do hours of work without us, making chat unnecessary for many tasks.

Over the past two months, I’ve recorded 788 voicemails for my agents; while on walks, riding the subway, or wherever I happen to be.

Behind the scenes, Claude Code runs on my laptop to ingest the voicemail transcripts and kick off work; fixing bugs, organizing ideas, starting research.

Later in the day I’ll check Claude’s work, record more thoughts, and go on with my day. More than 90% of my Claude Code sessions happen without me.

I want someone to solve my problems, not distract me with conversations.

Being an AI babysitter is easy; there’s no rejection and no failures a prompt can’t fix.

Shipping a product worth using is hard. Writing an essay you’re proud of is hard. Maintaining relationships is hard. Closing sales is hard. I’ve found AI can make these more elusive, warping my perspectives and eroding my conviction.

But it can also free us from the mundane and the tedious, leaving us space for the messy, hard work that needs doing.

title: Concord – Discord in Terminal
description: A feature-rich TUI client for Discord, written in Rust. - chojs23/concord
fullText:
Concord is a feature-rich TUI (terminal user interface) client for Discord, written in Rust with ratatui. Full Discord experience, right in your terminal.

Install native audio dependencies first. On macOS with Homebrew:

To install without local voice playback and microphone support:

To install the latest unreleased version directly from the Git repository:

Run without installing (requires flakes enabled):

Or add the flake as an input in your own flake.nix:

Install the latest release with the cargo-dist shell installer:

On Windows, use the PowerShell installer instead:

The installer places concord under $CARGO_HOME/bin, which is usually
~/.cargo/bin on Unix and %USERPROFILE%\.cargo\bin on Windows.

You need the Rust stable toolchain, Cargo, and the native dependencies listed in
the Cargo install section.

The release binary is produced at:

To build without local voice playback and microphone support, disable default features:

On WSLg, audio is usually exposed through PulseAudio instead of a real ALSA
sound card. If playback does not start, check that PulseAudio and ALSA routing
work before debugging Discord voice itself:

Email and QR code logins may trigger a CAPTCHA challenge on Discord's side. We cannot solve that, so I strongly recommend using token authentication.

Tokens are saved under Concord's config directory in plain text. See the Security section below for details.

Concord renders a practical subset of Discord-style Markdown in message bodies:

If your terminal does not support any graphics protocol, images will be rendered as halfblock approximations. For the best experience, use a terminal that supports the Kitty or iTerm2 protocol.

You can toggle image viewing on or off in the configuration file. When image viewing is off, attachments and emojis will be shown as text placeholders.

⚠️ Keymap action names and default bindings may have breaking changes between releases.

All default key settings in this section can be customized. See
Keymap options for the config format and supported
actions.

Concord has a four-pane.
Guilds (1), Channels (2), Messages (3), Members (4)

With default vim-style navigation:

Ctrl+n and Ctrl+p are fixed row movement keys. The default j and k
row movement keys are SelectNext and SelectPrevious and can be changed in
keymap.toml.

Press Space to open the leader shortcut window.

Focus a pane, then press Space, a to open actions for that pane. Action
shortcuts are shown inside the leader popup and only run when the action is
enabled. In the Messages pane, the selected message also supports these direct
shortcuts:

When the attachment viewer is open, press d to download the current attachment directly.

You can paste copied files into the composer to attach them. Pending uploads
are shown above the input before sending.

When the @mention picker is open, use Up / Down,
Ctrl+p / Ctrl+n, Tab, or Enter to choose a mention.

Type : plus at least two letters, such as :he, to pick Unicode, server, or
cross-server custom emoji while writing a message.

When emojis_as_links is enabled, custom emoji your account cannot send
directly are inserted as Discord image links instead.

To react from the composer, select a message, enter insert mode, then type
+:. The reaction picker opens for that message. Press / to search, Enter to
lock the filter, then Enter again or use a shown shortcut to react.

When the composer input starts with a slash /, the command suggestion popup

Concord options are stored under Concord's config directory. If
XDG_CONFIG_HOME is set, Concord uses $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/concord/config.toml
for app options and $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/concord/keymap.toml for key settings.
Otherwise it uses the platform config directory. The usual fallback is
~/.config/concord/config.toml and ~/.config/concord/keymap.toml on Linux,
matching files under ~/Library/Application Support/concord/ on macOS, and the
roaming AppData config directory on Windows.

You can change some configuration from the in-app Options menu, and Concord saves them back
to config.toml. Key settings are read from keymap.toml.

This setting only applies to attachment, embed, and attachment viewer previews.
Avatars and custom emoji keep their separate small-image behavior.

You can customize key bindings. Check the Keymap options for the config format, supported actions.

Concord is designed to stay lightweight in normal terminal use. In observed
typical use, it usually uses about 20-40 MB of memory.

bytes need to be decoded before they can be rendered in the terminal. When many

and then drop again as work completes and caches are pruned.

To keep resource usage bounded, Concord limits media work in several places:

Message history is also cached with a per-channel limit, so long-running
sessions do not keep every message in memory forever.

There are some path that did trigger a account block:

Other features have not caused blocks in my testing.

That said, Concord is not an official Discord client. Using unofficial clients, automated user accounts, or self-bots can violate Discord's TOS, so there is always some risk. Use it at your own discretion.

No. If Discord requires a CAPTCHA during login, use token login instead.

Any issues, pull requests, and feedback are welcome. See CONTRIBUTING.md for details.

Concord is licensed under GPL-3.0-only.

title: Samsung to Refund 20% of All Purchases in South Korea in Onuri Gift Certificates
description: Samsung Electronics will refund 20% of product purchases in Onnuri gift certificates from the 8th, the first step in its pledge of 5 trillion won in social contributions, with uniformed public servants receiving 30%.
fullText:
Samsung Electronics (005930.KS) said Thursday it will hold the "Together With the People, Appreciation Festival," refunding customers 20% of their total purchase amount in Onnuri gift certificates.

The event was arranged to share with the public the profits that have grown on the back of the semiconductor super cycle. When Samsung Electronics' senior management signed a tentative agreement on the 2026 wage accord with the labor union on Nov. 27, the company said it would create a fund worth 5 trillion won over the next five years to be used for social contribution activities.

Samsung Electronics is implementing the Appreciation Festival as its first measure for social contribution. The event, running from the 8th of this month to the 5th of next month, will provide 20% of the amount spent on smartphones, TVs and home appliances in Onnuri gift certificates. Public servants devoted to the nation, including soldiers, police, firefighters and correctional officers, will receive an additional 10 percentage points for a 30% refund benefit. "Through discussions with the board of directors and the compliance committee, we will review additional measures so that the management performance the company has achieved can lead to expanded social contribution," Samsung Electronics said.

Samsung Offers Onnuri Gift Certificates Instead of Price Discounts

Traditional Markets and Small Merchants Also Anticipate 'Trickle-Down Effect'

"We Hope Customer Benefits Lead to Expanded Local Sales"

The core of the "Together With the People, Appreciation Festival," which runs for four weeks starting on the 8th, is the method of the benefit. Samsung Electronics decided to provide Onnuri gift certificates instead of directly cutting product prices. The event was designed so that when customers purchase Samsung Electronics products, they receive a certain amount back in Onnuri gift certificates, which can then be used at traditional markets and local small-merchant stores. The idea is to create a structure in which the benefit of purchasing a large company's products does not end with the customer but flows through to the sales of local commercial districts.

Samsung Electronics expects that about 400 billion won worth of Onnuri gift certificates will be issued over one month. Onnuri gift certificates are a local currency introduced to revitalize traditional markets. Because their usage is limited to alley commercial districts and everyday-life stores, they are regarded as a representative consumption tool for expanding local spending. Samsung Electronics' use of Onnuri gift certificates in this event is interpreted as a choice aimed at recirculating consumption capacity into the local economy.

A Samsung Electronics official said, "We focused on ensuring that the benefits provided to customers can lead to expanded sales for local small merchants and small commercial districts," adding, "We are continuing to consider ways to share with society the achievements we have made through the public's support."

The Appreciation Festival is the first step among the concrete implementation measures of the "5 trillion won in social contributions over five years" pledge that Samsung Electronics recently announced. After the labor-management agreement last month, Samsung Electronics presented a policy to ensure that the company's growth and performance create a virtuous cycle not only for employees but also for society as a whole. At the time, the labor-management conflict over performance pay had become prolonged, increasing social fatigue, and the political sphere and the government had also urged a swift settlement. In response, Samsung Electronics expressed its intention to strengthen its social responsibility after the tentative agreement.

This Appreciation Festival was arranged amid such a flow. Samsung Electronics has publicly expressed its will to share the achievements made through the semiconductor super cycle not only as employee compensation but with the public, small merchants and the local economy.

Samsung Electronics' event is expected to provide a boost to domestic demand. Korea's economy recorded a real economic growth rate (seasonally adjusted) of 1.7% in the first quarter, higher than expected, but this was largely driven by a 5.1% increase in exports such as semiconductors from the previous quarter. By contrast, private consumption rose only 0.5% from the previous quarter, making the temperature gap between exports and domestic demand increasingly clear. Through the event refunding 20% of product purchase amounts in Onnuri gift certificates, Samsung Electronics plans to contribute to stimulating domestic consumption and revitalizing local commercial districts.

Notably, Samsung Electronics also significantly expanded benefits for uniformed public servants devoted to the nation. Marking June as the month of patriots and veterans, Samsung Electronics calls soldiers, police, firefighters and correctional officers "K-Heroes" and provides them with a 30% benefit on their purchase amount. The aim is to express gratitude to uniformed public servants who dedicate themselves to the safety of the nation and its people. The number of beneficiaries is estimated at more than 700,000, including military service members and civilian military personnel, police, firefighters and correctional officers.

Starting with this event, Samsung Electronics plans to specify additional social contribution measures such as support for partner companies, inclusive finance and AI talent development. It is also reviewing measures to enhance the competitiveness of the industrial ecosystem through partner company support and to help vulnerable groups with low financial access and small self-employed business owners become self-reliant. Cultivating talent in the AI field and expanding industry-academia cooperation are also cited as key tasks.

"We will continue to consider what role companies should play in our society, keeping in mind the public's expectations and standards," Samsung Electronics said, adding, "We will fulfill our responsibility as a company that grows together with society while contributing to national economic development through technological innovation and future investment."

Original reporting by Koo Kyung-woo for Seoul Economic Daily.

AI-translated from Korean. Quotes from foreign sources are based on Korean-language reports and may not reflect exact original wording.

title: Will club momentum boost NI's World Cup hopes?
description: With silverware fresh in their hands, many of Northern Ireland's players will arrive for international duty brimming with confidence.
fullText:
With silverware fresh in their hands, many of the Northern Ireland players will arrive for international duty with a confidence that only success brings.

In recent weeks, 10 members of Michael McArdle's 23-strong squad have either won league titles or secured promotion with their clubs.

Now they will aim to transfer that success to the international stage as Northern Ireland look to build momentum.

NI have already secured a World Cup play-off spot with two games to go in League B Group 2, but there is still plenty at stake against Turkey and Switzerland.

Northern Ireland come into this window buoyed by two positive results against Malta in April.

However, with several players involved in title triumphs that will live long in the memory, the side will have even greater belief.

Rebecca McKenna, Rebecca Holloway and Simone Magill, who missed most of the season through pregnancy but has since given birth, won promotion to the Women's Super League (WSL) with Birmingham City, while Ellie Mason was part of the Charlton Athletic squad who were the victors in the play-off to book a spot in the WSL

At Hearts, Joely Andrews, Lauren Wade and Keri Halliday, who spent the second half of the season on loan at Motherwell, won the Scottish Premiership title for the first time in the club's history.

Burnley secured promotion to Women's Super League 2 after an unbeaten league season with Brenna McPartlan, Danielle Maxwell and Louise McDaniel picking up a winner's medal.

Plenty of the players based in Northern Ireland have enjoyed recent success as well. Nadene Caldwell, Aimee Kerr and Mia Moore were all part of the Glentoran squad who lifted the Women's Premiership trophy last season and have recently begun their defence of the title.

McArdle believes the squad's success at club level can have a positive impact when they pull on a green shirt for Northern Ireland.

"We've got some very highly-confident players coming into camp with their achievements throughout the season. So, it's great for them individually," McArdle said.

"For some players, that promotion to WSL or WSL2 or wherever they go in their own individual journey... that could be great for them at that point in time. For others, it might not be what they need.

"So, it's really just the individual context. But certainly, with those players coming into camp on a high, it'll be great for us."

Forward Wade was part of the Northern Ireland squad who made history at the Euros in 2022.

Now, at club level, she helped Hearts win their first league title and secure European football.

"It was an amazing feeling what has happened with Hearts and now coming into an international window, it is nice to have that positivity and wind behind us," Wade told BBC Sport NI.

"You quickly have to shift that mentality and it is all about international football."

Wade believes the "spirits will be high" ahead of the final two group games, when NI travel to face Turkey on Friday (18:00 BST) before hosting group leaders Switzerland.

There is plenty on the line for the side, who know they must register positive results to avoid relegation to League C of the Nations League and will be aiming to leapfrog Friday's opponents and finish in second spot.

"It is brilliant to see what the girls are doing on the club stage and to have a few league titles and promotion," Wade added.

"We can only bring that into this camp and go off the previous camp as well and getting two wins against Malta, so the spirits will be high.

"We are wanting to be on the front foot and get two positive results - that is ultimately what we need."

title: We need to teach boys differently to girls to get best out of them - Southgate
description: The way boys are taught in schools should be changed to take account of their "fundamental differences" to girls, former England manager Sir Gareth Southgate says.
fullText:
The way boys are taught in schools should be changed to take account of their "fundamental differences" to girls, Sir Gareth Southgate has said.

The former England football manager told the BBC this was needed to tackle the crisis facing boys and young men who feel isolated and are struggling with education, employment, identity and mental health.

"I think anybody that's worked with either sex at a younger age knows there are some fundamental differences," he told BBC Radio 4's Today programme.

He said to get the most out of young people it "might require a slightly different approach when we're educating and developing young men, compared to educating and developing young women".

The 55-year-old has made a documentary for the BBC examining how young men struggling to find work, particularly those who did badly at school, can suffer a cascade of problems later on in life including poor mental health and loss of identity.

Gareth Southgate: Changing the Game for Young Men, which is released on Monday, also looks at possible solutions to the crisis, including older men volunteering to be mentors and encouraging more men to go into teaching.

The lack of role models for boys and young men was something Sir Gareth spoke of last year when he gave the BBC's annual Richard Dimbleby Lecture.

He criticised online influencers who were willing to manipulate and "trick young men into believing that success is measured by money or dominance".

Speaking to Today presenter Amol Rajan, Sir Gareth warned of historic expectations weighing on boys, and the compounding effect of poor attainment in school, leading to behavioural problems.

"That's leading to issues of being employable," he said, referencing a recent study by former Labour minister Alan Milburn about the level of young people not in education, employment or training (Neets). That affects both sexes, he added, but said there were "particular issues for boys".

Sir Gareth praised work to empower girls, saying "there's some brilliant work going on creating better opportunities for women" - and that trying to improve outcomes for boys was not a case of "either or".

"We've taken our eyes off some of the issues that men face through their lives and we have to start thinking about how we address those, as well as the issues for women and girls," he said.

The Milburn review came out last week alongside official figures showing more than one million young people were Neets - the highest level in more than 12 years.

Since the pandemic, the number of men aged 16-24 classified as Neet has risen by 40%. By comparison, it rose 7% for women.

For the documentary, Sir Gareth met three young men in Middlesbrough struggling to find work.

Taylor, 20, has been looking for a job for the last five months. He used to work in a warehouse but has struggled since then, particularly with the lack of communication from some employers.

Many do not inform candidates they have been rejected or provide feedback about why interviews have been unsuccessful, something highlighted in the Milburn report.

Joblessness caused Taylor to have feelings of worthlessness. "I just thought, 'I'm clearly not good enough, am I?'" He says he then turned to alcohol.

"I just wanted to go out, get drunk with my mates and pretty much forget about everything," he told Sir Gareth.

It reached the point where he was drinking most days and getting needlessly angry at his family and friends. He eventually "came to the conclusion that I didn't really want to be around anymore".

Taylor was able to change his mind and, in the documentary, Sir Gareth encouraged him to keep believing in himself and praised his strong communication skills.

That kind of advice and positive feedback is something the former England manager thinks young people need more of, telling Today he would like to see a "national movement where we're creating more mentors".

"There's a lot of people with experience who want to help," he said, adding the country wanted a "story of hope".

That feeling has been rising among England fans ahead of the World Cup, which features many players Sir Gareth brought through as manager.

"This group of players have now been through so many of the hurdles towards winning that we hadn't been through eight years ago," the former Three Lions defender said, adding he had "become an England fan again".

"Winning knockout matches, winning penalty shootouts, getting to finals... we will win a tournament at some point."

Gareth Southgate: Changing the Game for Young Men is available on BBC iPlayer from Monday morning and will air on BBC One at 21:00 BST on Monday.

If you have been affected by any of the issues in this article support is available at BBC Action Line.

title: The familiar phone call after Morrison's NI debut
description: Liverpool teenager Kieran Morrison said he was "buzzing" to make his senior Northern Ireland debut against Guinea on Thursday.
fullText:
Throughout his young career to date, Kieran Morrison takes a phone call from his dad after every game.

It was no different as he made his senior Northern Ireland debut against Guinea on Thursday.

Standing on the pitch in the sweltering La Linea sun, under the shadow of the Rock of Gibraltar, he spoke to his dad about his first 45 minutes in a green shirt.

"He was telling me what I could have done better and what I done well," a proud Morrison said.

"He does that every game. He's my coach and he has been since I was four years old.

"After every game he calls me until I answer. I'm glad I have that type of person in my corner, sometimes it's to give me criticism and other times it's to offer praise."

Despite only making two senior Liverpool appearances under the now-departed Arne Slot, in the EFL Cup and FA Cup, Morrison was called into Michael O'Neill's senior squad for the World Cup play-off with Italy in March.

He did not feature in Bergamo, or in the friendly with Wales that followed, but he got his chance in the 1-0 win over Guinea in Spain.

"At the start of the season I wouldn't have expected to hear that," he said on what it felt like to be called a Northern Ireland international.

"I'm very proud and my family is very proud too. I'm buzzing."

Atcheson scores and is sent off as NI beat Guinea

Wearing the number nine shirt, famous for David Healy's goalscoring exploits at the turn of the century, Morrison played the opening 45 minutes of the friendly against the African side.

With an average age of 22.1, he was part of the youngest starting XI on record since the end of World War Two, and he was one of two debutants.

Arsenal teenager Ceadach O'Neill was introduced off the bench, and Blackburn Rovers defender Tom Atcheson scored the winner but was also sent off as he made his first start.

"I was shocked when I seen number nine. I don't think I've ever worn number nine," Morrison said.

"I was expecting 10 or 17 or something, but I'm proud with number nine.

"I'm proud and I'll keep it forever."

He also said he was proud of his fellow debutant O'Neill, who also came through the Northern Ireland set up in the JD Academy.

"Me and Ceadach are close. I was mocking him on his number [O'Neill wore number 13], it's a goalkeeper's number.

"But I was proud of him to make his debut. He deserves it.

"It was a really proud moment for him and his family."

After getting a taste of international football, Morrison will be hoping for more minutes against France in Lille on Monday.

While Guinea pushed a young Northern Ireland side, France will be another level as their star-studded team will look to head off to the World Cup with a big win.

For Morrison, it will be a reunion with former Liverpool team-mate Ibrahima Konate, who looks set to join Real Madrid on a free transfer.

He joked that he is hoping their friendship will have some benefits when it comes to getting the shirt of a big name.

"It will be some game. Hopefully I can catch up with Ibou after it," Morrison said.

"That's the best game you can get. Hopefully I can get whatever minutes I can on the pitch and try to prove myself to the fans and for everyone.

"Hopefully I can tell him to get me a [Ousmane] Dembele or something on the secret. Hopefully I can get whatever shirt I can."
